_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Sets practice 1
questions.explanation: The `&` operator computes intersection, keeping only values present in both sets. The result remains a set, not a list.

questions.title: Sets practice 2
questions.prompt: Which expression finds courses completed by Ada but not Ben?
choices: ada - ben
ada | ben
ada & ben
ben - ada
questions.explanation: Set difference keeps members from the left set that are absent from the right. Here the result is `{"MATH120"}`.

questions.title: Trace Sets
questions.code:
courses = ["CS101", "MATH120", "CS101", "ENG101"]
unique_courses = set(courses)
print(len(unique_courses))
print("CS101" in unique_courses)
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: Repeated values disappear when the list becomes a set. The result has three unique course codes even though the source list has four items. The resulting output is '3\nTrue'.

questions.title: Sets good practice
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Sets?
choices: Use a set when duplicates have no meaning.
Skip input checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide every error and continue without checking the result.
Put all of the work in one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use a set when duplicates have no meaning. This keeps the code easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Sets practice 5
questions.prompt: Which version follows the lesson's safer approach?
choices:
clubs = {"Robotics"}
clubs.discard("Chess")

clubs = {"Robotics"}
clubs.remove("Chess")
Both versions are equally safe.
Neither version can run.
questions.explanation: `remove` raises KeyError when the value is absent. `discard` leaves the set unchanged, which is convenient when absence is acceptable.

questions.title: Sets practice 6
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains 'Curly braces have one empty exception'?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
`{"Python"}` is a set, but `{}` is an empty dictionary. Write `set()` when you need an empty set, then add values to it.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: `{"Python"}` is a set, but `{}` is an empty dictionary. Write `set()` when you need an empty set, then add values to it.

questions.title: Sets practice 7
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Sets?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Do not depend on item positions.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Do not depend on item positions. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Sets practice 8
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Sets?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
Prefer `discard` when a missing value is acceptable.
questions.explanation: Prefer `discard` when a missing value is acceptable. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Sets practice 9
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Sets?
choices: Use `&`, `|`, and `-` to compare groups.
Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Use `&`, `|`, and `-` to compare groups. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.

questions.title: Sets practice 10
questions.prompt: Which is a useful practice for Sets?
choices: Skip checks and assume every value is valid.
Create an empty set with `set()`.
Hide errors and continue without checking the result.
Put every step into one long statement.
questions.explanation: Create an empty set with `set()`. This makes the result easier to understand and verify.
